feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: PhD researcher at Laboratoire de Géologie, ENS Paris-PSL. My work focuses on the mechanical and thermal evolution of the lithosphere during continental rifting — combining geophysics, numerical modeling, and data-driven methods.

entry.title: Fieldwork in Tanzania — Chasing Tectonic Signals in the East African Rift

link.title: Fieldwork in Tanzania — Chasing Tectonic Signals in the East African Rift

entry.content:
In July 2025, I had the chance to join Prof. Eric Calais for a two-week field campaign in northern Tanzania — one of those rare opportunities where the data you collect ends up directly feeding your PhD thesis.

Why Tanzania?

The Natron Basin sits in the eastern branch of the East African Rift, one of the most active continental rift systems on Earth. It is where the African plate is slowly tearing itself apart — a process driven by the interplay of tectonic faulting and magmatic intrusions that we are still trying to fully understand.

My research focuses on how deformation accumulates between major rifting events. To quantify this, we need precise measurements of how the ground is moving — and that is exactly what GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) stations provide.

<!--
  See https://www.debugbear.com/blog/responsive-images#w-descriptors-and-the-sizes-attribute and
  https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Multimedia_and_embedding/Responsive_images for info on defining 'sizes' for responsive images
-->

  
    <source
      class="responsive-img-srcset"
      
        srcset="/assets/img/tanzania/photo1-480.webp 480w,/assets/img/tanzania/photo1-800.webp 800w,/assets/img/tanzania/photo1-1400.webp 1400w,"
        type="image/webp"
      
      
        sizes="95vw"
      
    >
  

<img
  src="/assets/img/tanzania/photo1.jpg"
  
    class="img-fluid rounded z-depth-1"
  
  
    width="100%"
  
  
    height="auto"
  
  
  
  
  
  
    loading="eager"
  
  onerror="this.onerror=null; $('.responsive-img-srcset').remove();"
>

</picture>

<figcaption class="caption">Your caption here</figcaption>

</figure> –>

The Campaign

From July 16 to 30, our team deployed and maintained a network of campaign GNSS/GPS stations across the Natron Basin. The work was made possible with the support of Dr. Elifuraha Saria from Ardhi University in Dar es Salaam and Valerie Mackie, whose local expertise and logistical help were invaluable.

[Add a few sentences about the day-to-day experience — driving across the basin, setting up stations, challenges encountered, landscapes, wildlife, etc.]

<!--
  See https://www.debugbear.com/blog/responsive-images#w-descriptors-and-the-sizes-attribute and
  https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Multimedia_and_embedding/Responsive_images for info on defining 'sizes' for responsive images
-->

  
    <source
      class="responsive-img-srcset"
      
        srcset="/assets/img/tanzania/photo2-480.webp 480w,/assets/img/tanzania/photo2-800.webp 800w,/assets/img/tanzania/photo2-1400.webp 1400w,"
        type="image/webp"
      
      
        sizes="95vw"
      
    >
  

<img
  src="/assets/img/tanzania/photo2.jpg"
  
    class="img-fluid rounded z-depth-1"
  
  
    width="100%"
  
  
    height="auto"
  
  
  
  
  
  
    loading="eager"
  
  onerror="this.onerror=null; $('.responsive-img-srcset').remove();"
>

</picture>

<figcaption class="caption">Your caption here</figcaption>

</figure> –>

What We Measured

At each station, we collected high-precision horizontal (and vertical) velocity data. These velocities represent the integrated motion of the crust over the observation period — essentially, how fast and in which direction the ground is moving relative to stable Africa.

By comparing these geodetic velocities with seismic moment rates derived from earthquake catalogs, we can estimate what fraction of the ongoing deformation is released seismically and how much is accommodated through aseismic processes — things like slow fault creep, distributed deformation, or magma movement at depth.

[Add any specific observation or highlight from the data collection that you found interesting or surprising.]

<!--
  See https://www.debugbear.com/blog/responsive-images#w-descriptors-and-the-sizes-attribute and
  https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Multimedia_and_embedding/Responsive_images for info on defining 'sizes' for responsive images
-->

  
    <source
      class="responsive-img-srcset"
      
        srcset="/assets/img/tanzania/photo3-480.webp 480w,/assets/img/tanzania/photo3-800.webp 800w,/assets/img/tanzania/photo3-1400.webp 1400w,"
        type="image/webp"
      
      
        sizes="95vw"
      
    >
  

<img
  src="/assets/img/tanzania/photo3.jpg"
  
    class="img-fluid rounded z-depth-1"
  
  
    width="100%"
  
  
    height="auto"
  
  
  
  
  
  
    loading="eager"
  
  onerror="this.onerror=null; $('.responsive-img-srcset').remove();"
>

</picture>

<figcaption class="caption">Your caption here</figcaption>

</figure> –>

Beyond the Science

[This is a good place for a personal paragraph — what it felt like to be there, something unexpected that happened, a memorable moment, the food, the people, the landscape. Make it human.]

What Comes Next

The velocities collected during this campaign are now being processed and will directly inform the strain partitioning analysis that forms the core of my first PhD chapter. Early results were presented at EGU General Assembly 2026 — and there is more to come.

This fieldwork was conducted as part of my PhD at the Laboratoire de Géologie, ENS Paris-PSL, within the TALENTS doctoral network.
entry.summary: Two weeks in the Natron Basin deploying and maintaining GNSS stations to capture present-day crustal deformation.
